context.project_description: Implement token usage tracking for LLM calls across multiple providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) to provide visibility into token consumption per query for billing and compliance purposes.
key_requirements: Add token counting capabilities to all LLM provider classes
Integrate with existing instrumentation system using BenchmarkHook protocol
Track tokens consumed per LLM call (prompt/completion) for chat, embedding, rerank, and vision providers
Include token usage metadata in Answer objects for end-user visibility
Support provider-specific tokenization methods (tiktoken, Anthropic's count_tokens, etc.)
constraints: All implementation must use existing codebase patterns and protocols
Token counting logic must be provider-specific due to different tokenization approaches
Integration with InstrumentedProxy system must maintain existing behavior
Changes must not break existing functionality or APIs
stakeholders: LLM cost tracking user who needs visibility into token consumption per query
Billing system that requires accurate token counts for cost calculation
Compliance team needing precise usage data for audit purposes

in_scope: Token counting logic with provider-specific implementations
Instrumentation hook to track LLM calls and record tokens consumed
Data structures to hold token usage information including per-query or global aggregation
Extension of base protocols with token-related capabilities
Enhancement of specific LLM provider client classes (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama) with token tracking
out_of_scope: Aggregated usage per query instead of individual call counts
Universal tokenizer that works across all providers
Approximate token values for logging only purposes
Token counting for non-LLM components or external services
UI/UX changes to display token usage in user-facing interfaces

existing_files: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py — Provider protocols for LLM clients.
fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py — Instrumentation system for benchmarking plugin performance.
needed_artifacts: fitz_ai/llm/token_counter.py — Core token counting logic with provider-specific implementations
fitz_ai/core/token_usage.py — Data structures to hold token usage information
fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py — Enhance OpenAI client classes with token tracking
fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py — Enhance Anthropic client classes with token tracking
fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py — Enhance Cohere client classes with token tracking
fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py — Enhance Ollama client classes with token tracking

assumptions.assumption: Different LLM providers have their own tokenization methods and must be implemented per provider.
assumptions.impact: We might try a universal tokenizer, which would introduce inaccuracies for various models.

assumptions.assumption: The tracking should happen at the LLM client level (per call), not higher-level engine logic.
assumptions.impact: There may be use cases where aggregated usage per query makes more sense than individual call counts.

assumptions.assumption: Accurate token count is required, especially for cost tracking or compliance purposes.
assumptions.impact: If approximate values suffice (e.g., logging only), we can simplify the implementation significantly.

assumptions.assumption: Each provider requires its own token counting logic due to different tokenization methods (tiktoken, etc.).
assumptions.impact: A shared tokenizer or library exists that works across providers — but this is unlikely in practice.

assumptions.assumption: Token usage should be included within the `Answer` object’s metadata for visibility to end users.
assumptions.impact: We might expose it via logs, separate API endpoints, or dashboards instead.

assumptions.assumption: For streaming chat methods (`chat_stream`), we should track total tokens including the response stream.
assumptions.impact: Maybe only prompt tokens are relevant and completion tokens from stream can be ignored or tracked separately.

approaches.name: Augment Return Types
approaches.description: Modify provider interfaces to return extended data structures containing both content and metadata, updating Answer to support token tracking through optional fields.
pros: Maintains backward compatibility through optional fields
Enables instrumentation to extract usage without changing hook signatures
Follows existing patterns in the codebase where providers can extend return types beyond protocol definitions
cons: Requires changes to multiple provider implementations
May introduce breaking changes for consumers expecting simple string returns
best_for: Systems requiring full observability with minimal disruption
When interface modification is acceptable and forward-compatible design is desired

approaches.name: Side-Channel Tracking
approaches.description: Store tokens in thread-local storage during provider execution and create monitoring hooks that read from this context, avoiding changes to return types or interfaces.
pros: No modification to existing provider contracts or Answer data structure required
cons: Introduces non-deterministic behavior due to thread-local storage
Increases coupling between instrumentation and implementation details
Harder to reason about correctness compared to explicit return values
best_for: When interface changes are strictly prohibited
Systems with high risk of breaking existing consumers

architecture.reasoning: The Augment Return Types approach is right because it directly addresses the architectural gap using the existing infrastructure capabilities. The InstrumentedProxy already supports arbitrary return types as demonstrated by its _wrap_method function that passes results unmodified to hooks. Provider interfaces don't enforce scalar returns - they are just type hints, and implementations can extend beyond these contracts. The Answer dataclass is a simple container where adding optional token_usage field maintains backward compatibility while enabling feature completeness. In contrast, Side-Channel Tracking introduces unnecessary complexity through thread-local storage and increases coupling between instrumentation and implementation details without providing better correctness guarantees.

key_tradeoffs.augment_return_types_vs_side_channel_tra: Augmenting return types enables clean metadata extraction and backward compatibility through optional fields, but requires interface changes; side-channel tracking avoids breaking changes but introduces non-deterministic behavior and tight coupling.

technology_considerations: Python protocols do not enforce return type restrictions at runtime, only static typing hints
InstrumentedProxy can handle arbitrary return types since it passes the actual result to hooks without assumptions about structure
BenchmarkHook.on_call_end signature already supports generic 'result' parameter for metadata extraction
Thread-local storage could enable side-channel tracking but introduces complexity and non-determinism
architecture.scope_statement: Extend provider interfaces to optionally return metadata alongside content, update the Answer dataclass to support token usage tracking, and modify instrumentation hooks to extract and aggregate this information during LLM calls.

adrs.title: ADR: Provider Interfaces Support Rich Return Types for Metadata
adrs.context: The analysis disproves claims that LLM provider interfaces enforce scalar returns and that instrumentation cannot extract metadata from complex objects. The current architecture allows for flexible return types through protocols, enabling richer data like token usage to be returned.
adrs.decision: Provider interfaces in fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py should be updated to allow rich return types (e.g., tuples or custom dataclasses) that can include metadata such as token usage alongside core outputs. This maintains backward compatibility while supporting enhanced observability.
adrs.rationale: Protocols define method signatures but do not restrict actual implementation behavior. The current InstrumentedProxy system already supports arbitrary return types, and hooks can access full results including metadata. Extending interfaces to optionally support richer returns enables token tracking without breaking changes.
consequences: Backward compatibility maintained through optional extended interfaces
Instrumentation system gains capability to extract usage information from provider responses
Answer dataclass must be updated to include token_usage field for storing metadata
Provider implementations will need to evolve to return structured data when possible
alternatives_considered: Alternative: Side-channel tracking through thread-local storage - rejected because it introduces non-deterministic behavior and tight coupling between instrumentation and implementation details
Alternative: Modify Answer directly without changing provider interfaces - rejected because this approach doesn't address the root issue of where usage data originates and how to capture it

adrs.title: ADR: Token Usage Tracking Through Instrumentation Hooks
adrs.context: The analysis demonstrates that instrumentation system does not assume scalar returns but rather passes full results to hooks, making it possible to extract metadata from complex provider responses.
adrs.decision: Instrumentation hooks should be enhanced to inspect and aggregate token usage data from provider responses via the existing `on_call_end` interface which already accepts generic result objects.
adrs.rationale: The benchmark hook interface in fitz_ai/core/instrumentation.py already supports arbitrary result types. This makes it feasible to extract metadata like token usage without requiring changes to core interfaces, leveraging existing mechanisms for observability.
consequences: No breaking changes required to provider interfaces or core engine logic
Instrumentation becomes capable of extracting and aggregating usage statistics automatically
Hook implementation must be updated to recognize and process token-related fields in results
Observability infrastructure gains visibility into actual LLM resource consumption
alternatives_considered: Alternative: Create entirely new hook mechanism for token tracking - rejected because it duplicates existing functionality and increases complexity unnecessarily
Alternative: Force all providers to implement special token-aware wrappers - rejected because this adds unnecessary overhead and coupling across the system

phases.name: Read Before Writing
phases.objective: Verify integration points by reading source
deliverables: Verified: fitz_ai/llm/providers/base.py
Verified: fitz_ai/llm/providers/openai.py
Verified: fitz_ai/llm/providers/anthropic.py
Verified: fitz_ai/llm/providers/cohere.py
Verified: fitz_ai/llm/providers/ollama.py
phases.verification_command: pytest fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_base.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_openai.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_anthropic.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_cohere.py fitz_ai/llm/providers/test_ollama.py

phases.name: Modify Provider Classes
phases.objective: Add token counting capability to all LLM provider implementations (OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Ollama)
deliverables: Updated OpenAIChat to capture input/output tokens from API responses
Updated AnthropicChat to capture input/output tokens from API responses
Updated CohereChat to capture input/output tokens from API responses
Updated OllamaChat to capture input/output tokens from HTTP responses
key_risks: Providers may not expose token usage in response objects
API changes could break token extraction logic

phases.name: Create Token Counter Registry
phases.objective: Implement a centralized mechanism to track and store token usage per query
deliverables: TokenCounter class with methods for tracking input/output tokens
Registry that stores token counts by query identifier
key_risks: Race conditions in concurrent request handling
Memory overhead from storing large amounts of token data

phases.name: Integrate Token Tracking into Engine Flow
phases.objective: Hook the token counter into the main engine execution path to capture usage during query processing
deliverables: Modified FitzKragEngine.answer() to use token counter
Updated SDK methods (fitz.ask, fitz.query) to expose token counts in responses
key_risks: Performance impact on query latency due to token tracking overhead
Incompatibility with existing answer schema if not carefully implemented

risks.description: LLM providers do not currently return token usage metrics in their responses, requiring modification of each provider implementation to capture input/output tokens.
risks.mitigation: Modify each ChatProvider class (OpenAIChat, AnthropicChat, etc.) to wrap API calls and extract token counts from response metadata.
risks.contingency: Implement a fallback mechanism that estimates token usage based on character count or uses approximate tokenization libraries as backup.
risks.verification: Verify that each provider's chat method returns both content and token count after modification

risks.description: Introducing token tracking requires changes to existing provider interfaces without breaking backward compatibility.
risks.mitigation: Use wrapper functions or decorators instead of modifying base protocols to preserve interface contracts.
risks.contingency: Maintain original return signatures while adding optional token count parameters in a way that doesn't break existing code.
risks.verification: Ensure all provider methods still work as before when token tracking is disabled

risks.description: Developing and maintaining custom wrappers or decorators for multiple LLM providers increases development overhead.
risks.mitigation: Create reusable base classes or utility functions that can be inherited/shared across provider implementations.
risks.contingency: Use a single generic decorator pattern that works with any ChatProvider to reduce duplication and maintenance burden.
risks.verification: Confirm all providers use the same token tracking mechanism without redundant code

risks.description: Token estimation accuracy may vary between different LLMs and models, potentially leading to inconsistent reporting.
risks.mitigation: Implement model-specific token estimators where available or use official tokenizer libraries for precise counts.
risks.contingency: Log warnings when token estimation is approximate and provide fallback mechanisms like character-based approximations.
risks.verification: Validate that token counts match expected values from actual API responses where available

recommended_contingencies: Implement a fallback mechanism that estimates token usage based on character count or approximate tokenizer libraries
Maintain original return signatures while adding optional token count parameters to preserve backward compatibility
Create reusable base classes for token tracking wrappers to reduce duplication across providers

Incompatibility with existing answer schema if not carefully implemented

risks.description: LLM providers do not currently return token usage metrics in their responses, requiring modification of each provider implementation to capture input/output tokens.
risks.mitigation: Modify each ChatProvider class (OpenAIChat, AnthropicChat, etc.) to wrap API calls and extract token counts from response metadata.
risks.contingency: Implement a fallback mechanism that estimates token usage based on character count or uses approximate tokenization libraries as backup.
risks.verification: Verify that each provider's chat method returns both content and token count after modification

risks.description: Introducing token tracking requires changes to existing provider interfaces without breaking backward compatibility.
risks.mitigation: Use wrapper functions or decorators instead of modifying base protocols to preserve interface contracts.
risks.contingency: Maintain original return signatures while adding optional token count parameters in a way that doesn't break existing code.
risks.verification: Ensure all provider methods still work as before when token tracking is disabled

risks.description: Developing and maintaining custom wrappers or decorators for multiple LLM providers increases development overhead.
risks.mitigation: Create reusable base classes or utility functions that can be inherited/shared across provider implementations.
risks.contingency: Use a single generic decorator pattern that works with any ChatProvider to reduce duplication and maintenance burden.
risks.verification: Confirm all providers use the same token tracking mechanism without redundant code

risks.description: Token estimation accuracy may vary between different LLMs and models, potentially leading to inconsistent reporting.
risks.mitigation: Implement model-specific token estimators where available or use official tokenizer libraries for precise counts.
risks.contingency: Log warnings when token estimation is approximate and provide fallback mechanisms like character-based approximations.
risks.verification: Validate that token counts match expected values from actual API responses where available

recommended_contingencies: Implement a fallback mechanism that estimates token usage based on character count or approximate tokenizer libraries
Maintain original return signatures while adding optional token count parameters to preserve backward compatibility
Create reusable base classes for token tracking wrappers to reduce duplication across providers
